abstract:
Advancements in membrane science have expanded the scope and necessity for innovative, high-performance separation materials that combine efficiency, chemical resistance, and extreme specialization. A particularly significant frontier is the selective separation of rare earth elements (REEs). Given their critical importance to modern high-tech industries—ranging from electric vehicle motors to wind turbines—the global demand for raw REEs has surged. This urgency is underscored by the classification of REE isolation as one of the seven chemical separation processes that, if optimized, would yield profound global benefits.

Membrane-based strategies are increasingly favored for metal recovery and wastewater treatment as sustainable, "green" alternatives characterized by operational simplicity. While various methodologies for REE separation have emerged, the existing literature reveals a need for comprehensive reviews that focus specifically on non-liquid membrane systems. Historically, liquid membranes were common but were frequently plagued by issues regarding long-term stability, durability, and leaching of extractants. Consequently, the field is transitioning toward more robust, non-liquid architectures where carriers are physically or chemically anchored to the membrane matrix or support structure. This shift eliminates the risk of extractant loss into the aqueous phase, enhancing both stability and performance.

This review examines current membrane-based REE separation methods—with a focus on imprinted, polymer inclusion, nanocomposite, and framework-based (MOF/COF) membranes—while evaluating essential technical metrics such as extraction efficiency, transport kinetics, and long-term stability.
